title: The Sleeping Beauty
content:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Call that a yarn!ן¿½ said old Tom Pugh,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½What rot! Iן¿½ll lay my hat
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Iן¿½ll sling you a yarn worth more nor two
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Such pumped-up yarns as that.ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And thereupon old Tommy ן¿½slewן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ A yarn of Lambing Flat.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½When Lambing Flat broke out,ן¿½ he said,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½ן¿½Mongst others there I knew
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ A lanky, orkard, Lunnon-bred
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Young chap named Johnny Drew,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And nicknamed for his love of bed,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The ן¿½Sleeping Beautyן¿½ too.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½He sunk a duffer on the Flat,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ In compן¿½ny with three more,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And makinן¿½ room for this and that
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ They was a tidy four,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Save when the eldest, Dublin Pat,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Got drunk and raved for gore.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½This Jack at yarninן¿½ licked a book,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And half the night heן¿½d spout.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But when he once turned in, it took
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Old Nick to get him out.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And that is how they came to cook
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The joke I tell about.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½A duffer-rush broke out one day,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I quite forget where at
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ (It doesnן¿½t matter, anyway,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ It didnן¿½t feed a cat),
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And Johnnieן¿½s party said theyן¿½d say
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Good-bye to Lambing Flat.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Next mom rose Johnnieן¿½s mates to pack
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And make an early shunt,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But all they could get out of Jack
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Was ן¿½All right,ן¿½ or a grunt,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ By pourinן¿½ water down his back
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And, when he turned, his front.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½The billy biled, the tea was made,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ They sat and ate their fill,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But Jack upon his broad back laid,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Snored like a fog-horn still;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Weן¿½ll save some tea to scald him,ן¿½ said
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The peaceful Corney Bill.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½As they their beef and damper ate
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And swilled their pints of tea
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ A bully notion all at wonst
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Dawned on that rowdy three.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And Dublin Pat, in frantic mirth,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Said, ן¿½Now weן¿½ll have a spree!ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Well, arter that, Iן¿½m safe to swear,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The beggars didnן¿½t lag,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But packed their togs with haste and care,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And each one made his swag
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ With Johnnieן¿½s moleskins, evן¿½ry pair,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Included in the bag.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½With nimble fingers from the pegs
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ They soon the strings unbent,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And off its frame as sure as eggs
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ They drew the blessed tent,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And rolled it up and stretched their legs,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And packed the lot, and went.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½And scarcely pן¿½rן¿½aps a thing to love,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The ן¿½Beautyן¿½ slumbered sound,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ With nought but Heavenן¿½s blue above
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And Lambing Flat around,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Until in sight some diggers hove,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Some diggers outן¿½ard bound.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½They sez as twelve oן¿½clock was nigh,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Weן¿½ll say for sure elevן¿½n,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ When Johnnie opeן¿½d his right-hand eye
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And looked straight up to Heavן¿½n:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I reckon he got more surprise
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Than struck the fabled Sevן¿½n.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Clean off his bunk he made a bound,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And when he rubbed his eyes
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Iן¿½m safe to swear poor Johnnie found
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ His dander ן¿½gin to rise.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ For there were diggers standinן¿½ round,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Their missuses likewise.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½O Lorן¿½! the joke, it wasnן¿½t lost,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Though it did well-nigh tear
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The sides of them as came acrost
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The flat to hear Jack swear,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ They sez as how old Grimshaw tossed
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ His grey wig in the air.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Some minutes on the ground Jack lay,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And bore their screaminן¿½ jeers,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And every bloke that passed that way
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Contributed his sneers;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Jack cursed aloud, that cursed day
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Seemed lengthened into years.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Then in a fury up he sprung,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ A pretty sight, you bet,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And laid about him with his tongue
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Advising us ן¿½to getן¿½,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And praying we might all be hung,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I think I hear him yet.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Then on a sudden, down he bent,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And grabbed a chunk of rock,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And into Grimshawן¿½s stomach sent
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The fossil, with a shock,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And Grimshaw doubled up and went
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To pieces with the knock.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½And in the sun that day Jack stood
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Clad only in his shirt,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And fired with stones and bits of wood,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And with his tongue threw dirt;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He fought as long as eן¿½er he could,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But very few were hurt.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½He stooped to tear a lump of schist
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Out of the clinging soil,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ By thunder, you should hear him jist,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And see the way heן¿½d coil
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Upon the ground, and hug his fist,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And scratch and dig and toil!

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½ן¿½Tis very plain heן¿½d struck it fat,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The dufferinן¿½ Lunnon muff;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The scoff and butt of Lambing Flat,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Who always got it rough,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Could strike his fortune where he sat;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The joker held the stuff.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Well, thatן¿½s the yarn, it ainן¿½t so poor;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Them golden days is oן¿½er,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And Dublin Pat was drowned, and sure
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ It quenched his thirst for gore;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Old Corney Bill and Dave the Cure
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I never heard on more.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½The Sleepinן¿½ Beautyן¿½s wealthy, too,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And wears a shiny hat,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But often comes to old Tom Pugh
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To have a quiet chat;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I lent him pants to get him through
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ His fix on Lambing Flat.ן¿½
meaning: About: Henry Lawson (17 June 1867 - 2 September 1922) was an Australian writer and poet. Along with his contemporary Banjo Paterson, Lawson is among the best-known Australian poets and fiction writers of the colonial period.
